report:
## Profitability

PepsiCo and Coca-Cola present contrasting profitability trajectories through FY2025. PEP operates at roughly twice KO's revenue scale — FY2025 revenues of ~$93.9B [PEP:Q3] versus KO's ~$47.9B [KO:Q3] — yet KO's more asset-light, concentrate-focused model generates superior margin ratios. KO's gross margin in FY2025 was approximately 61.6% ($29.5B gross profit [KO:Q15] on $47.9B revenue [KO:Q3]), while PEP's was roughly 55.4% ($50.9B [PEP:Q15] on $93.9B [PEP:Q3]). The divergence is even sharper at the net income line: KO earned $13.1B net income [KO:Q7] in FY2025 on a smaller revenue base, surpassing PEP's $8.2B [PEP:Q7] — implying a net margin of ~27% for KO versus ~8.8% for PEP. KO's operating income also surged to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11], well above PEP's $11.5B [PEP:Q11], which declined from its FY2024 peak of $12.9B [PEP:Q10]. PEP's profitability appears to be under pressure in FY2025, with both operating and net income retreating from FY2024 highs, whereas KO posted its strongest results across the period.

**PEP:** PepsiCo grew revenues steadily from $86.4B in FY2022 [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3], but profitability peaked in FY2024 — operating income hit $12.9B [PEP:Q10] and net income reached $9.6B [PEP:Q6] — before retreating to $11.5B [PEP:Q11] and $8.2B [PEP:Q7] respectively in FY2025, signaling meaningful margin compression. Notably, gross profit continued to grow to $50.9B [PEP:Q15], suggesting cost pressures are concentrated below the gross line, likely in SG&A or restructuring charges.

**KO:** Coca-Cola delivered its strongest profitability in FY2025, with operating income of $13.8B [KO:Q11] and net income of $13.1B [KO:Q7] — both well above any prior year in the dataset — on revenues of $47.9B [KO:Q3]. Its gross margin has expanded consistently, reaching ~61.6% in FY2025 [KO:Q15, KO:Q3], and its net margin of roughly 27% reflects the high-return nature of its franchise and concentrate model.

---

KO's operating income also surged to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11], well above PEP's $11.5B [PEP:Q11], which actually declined from its FY2024 peak of $12.9B [PEP:Q10]. PEP's profitability appears to be under pressure in FY2025, with both operating and net income retreating from FY2024 highs, whereas KO posted its strongest results across the period.

quality.notes: The report is exceptionally clear and well-structured, with a logical flow from headline comparison to company-specific breakdowns. The side-by-side profitability analysis is thorough and uses precise figures with consistent citation tags. Margin calculations are shown explicitly, aiding transparency. The observation that PEP's gross profit grew while operating/net income fell — pointing to below-gross-line cost pressures — demonstrates genuine analytical depth rather than surface-level data reporting. The honesty score is a 4 rather than 5 because the report does not flag any potential data limitations, such as whether FY2025 figures are full-year or annualized from partial-year data (the citation tags reference Q3, which could imply trailing or partial figures), and it does not caveat the KO net income figure, which at ~27% net margin is unusually high and may reflect non-recurring items worth flagging. A brief acknowledgment of these uncertainties would have earned a perfect score on that dimension.
